content_en:
Al-Kasani's prayer chapter in Bada'i as-Sana'i is organized with his characteristic systematic framework: the definition of prayer, its types, its conditions (shurut), its pillars (arkan), its obligatory elements (wajibat), its sunnahs, and its nullifiers. This organization allows the reader to immediately locate the legal status of any element of prayer and to understand how the different levels of obligation relate to each other.

Al-Kasani distinguishes clearly between the conditions of prayer (shurut as-sihha, conditions for validity) and the pillars (arkan, the constitutive acts). The conditions must be present before the prayer begins; the pillars constitute the prayer's substance. Conditions include taharah, covering the 'awrah, facing the qiblah, entering the prayer time, and intention. The pillar of intention is treated by al-Kasani as technically a condition that coincides with the opening act — a subtle but important point of Hanafi legal analysis.

The Hanafi school's approach to the opening takbir is more restrictive than other schools: the words must be 'Allahu Akbar' specifically — no synonyms or translations are valid. Al-Kasani defends this position with the principle that worship is defined by the Shari'ah's specific forms, not by the underlying meaning, and the opening of prayer has been defined by the prophetic practice as these specific words. This strictness in the formulaic elements of worship is a characteristic Hanafi position.

The chapter on what constitutes the follower's relationship to the imam — and what actions by the imam the follower must follow — is treated with the precision characteristic of Bada'i as-Sana'i. Al-Kasani presents the principle that the follower's prayer is 'connected' (muta'alliq) to the imam's prayer such that the imam's actions are actions of the follower by proxy. This explains why an error by the imam that would normally require sujud as-sahw is covered by the imam's own sujud as-sahw — the follower need not perform their own.

For travelers' prayer, al-Kasani addresses the question of why the traveler shortens the prayer rather than merely being permitted to. His answer is instructive: the shortened prayer (qasr) is the full prayer for the traveler, not a concession from the full prayer. Allah has prescribed two rak'ahs for the traveler just as He has prescribed four for the resident — neither is more 'complete' than the other in its context. This theological point, grounded in the Quranic verse 'there is no sin upon you if you shorten the prayer when you travel' (4:101), helps explain why the Prophet consistently prayed qasrsalah during travel without exception.

The chapter on prayer nullifiers includes al-Kasani's careful analysis of speech during prayer. The Hanafi school holds that intentional human speech (kalam al-adami) — even a single intelligible utterance — nullifies prayer regardless of content. If a person says 'subhanallah' intending to warn someone of danger rather than as a dhikr, their prayer is nullified. Al-Kasani defends this strict position with the principle that prayer is exclusively a relationship with Allah, and any human-directed communication — regardless of the words used — disrupts that relationship.
content_id:
Bab shalat al-Kasani dalam Bada'i as-Sana'i disusun dengan kerangka sistematis khasnya: definisi shalat, jenis-jenisnya, syarat-syaratnya (syuruth), rukun-rukunnya (arkan), kewajiban-kewajibannya (wajibat), perbuatan-perbuatan sunnah, dan pembatal-pembatalnya. Kerangka berlapis ini memungkinkan al-Kasani menyajikan hukum shalat yang luas dengan cara yang memudahkan pelajar menemukan hukum yang tepat untuk setiap pertanyaan.

Syarat-syarat shalat mendapat perlakuan terperinci: thaharah dari hadats dan najis, menutup aurat, menghadap kiblat, waktu, niat. Al-Kasani menjelaskan apa yang dibutuhkan oleh masing-masing syarat ini dengan cara yang memperjelas kondisi-kondisi batas: kapan thaharah seseorang dianggap cukup, sampai sejauh mana pakaian harus menutup aurat, apa yang diperlukan oleh niat. Keterperincian ini menjadikan Bada'i lebih berguna untuk penentuan hukum praktis daripada kebanyakan manual yang lebih ringkas.

Rukun-rukun shalat — takbir pembuka, berdiri bagi yang mampu, bacaan Al-Fatihah, ruku, sujud, dan tasyahud akhir — dibedakan dengan jelas dari tindakan-tindakan wajib yang bila ditinggalkan mengharuskan sujud sahwi, dan keduanya dibedakan dari sunnah-sunnah yang penghilangan-nya tidak memengaruhi keabsahan shalat. Hierarki ini sangat penting bagi pelajar fikih dan disajikan dalam Bada'i dengan kejelasan yang jarang ditandingi dalam literatur Hanafi klasik.
